slides.title: The quiet exercise that *lowers blood pressure*.
slides.sub: Holding still under tension may move resting blood pressure more than steady cardio. The move is the wall sit.

slides.title: Isometric holds, not reps
slides.body: An isometric hold means you contract a muscle without moving. A wall sit is the classic example: knees bent, back flat, *holding still*.

slides.title: Tension trains your vessels
slides.body: Sustained tension briefly limits blood flow, then floods it back. That cycle *can train your arteries* to relax and widen over time.

slides.title: Stronger than the cardio myth
slides.body: Reviews suggest isometric training *may lower resting blood pressure* more than running or cycling for many people. It is a surprising result.

slides.title: Four holds, twice a week
slides.body: A common approach is four two-minute holds with rest between, a few days a week. Small, *steady, and easy to keep*.

slides.title: Consistency is the real lever
slides.body: The benefit fades if you stop. Like most longevity habits, it *only pays off when repeated* week after week. Tracking helps it stick.

slides.title: Turn small habits into *real progress*.
slides.body: Vibe Health logs your movement, sleep, food and steps, then rolls them into one daily Health Score so your routine actually sticks.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
A two-minute wall sit might do more for your blood pressure than a long run. 🧱🌿

Isometric holds train your arteries to relax, four holds a few days a week can lower resting blood pressure, and the only catch is staying consistent. Save this. 🔖
